sections.vendors: Sainsbury's TU sources from 300+ sites across 15 countries, with dedicated ethical trade teams operating from UK, Hong Kong, Shanghai, Dhaka and Delhi offices. No new supplier additions or removals announced this week. TU's Supplier Code of Conduct remains aligned with ETI Base Code across all 300 sites.
sections.sustainability: TU has switched plastic babywear hangers to cardboard — a packaging sustainability win that signals direction of travel for other garment categories. Sainsbury's maintains its Scope 3 emission reduction target of 30% by 2030, measured using the Higg Index across key raw material categories.
sections.sourcing: TU sources 80% of its high summer 2026 womenswear collection at £25 or under — indicating continued value-first sourcing discipline. No nearshoring or sourcing country changes announced. Bangladesh and China remain primary manufacturing hubs per Dhaka and Shanghai office operations.
sections.strategy: TU has locked school uniform prices for a fourth consecutive year — a strong value positioning move ahead of back-to-school season. The high summer collection launched with phased drops from May to August 2026, indicating a move away from two-season buying toward continuous newness.
sections.pricing: 80% of high summer 2026 womenswear priced at £25 or less — TU is doubling down on budget credentials against George and Tesco F&F. School uniform price lock (4th year running) reinforces value message to families.
sections.launches: TU's high summer womenswear 2026 collection is live — phased drops from May through August with a mix of occasionwear and casual staples. Babywear sustainability upgrade (cardboard hangers replacing plastic) rolled out across all TU baby ranges in store.
sections.gossip: TU's continuous drop model (May–August phases) rather than a single summer range mirrors what ASOS does digitally — unusual for a supermarket brand and suggests TU's buying team is operating more like a fashion brand than a grocery sideline.
soWhat: School uniform price lock 4th year running means TU buyers will resist any price increases on jersey schoolwear — keep school polo and trouser FOBs flat or offer volume discounts to retain the programme.
Phased summer collection drops signal that TU is moving toward faster replenishment cycles — suppliers need shorter lead times and more flexibility rather than one large order per season.
Plastic to cardboard packaging shift on babywear will likely extend to other categories — start offering cardboard hang-tag and hanger solutions proactively to stay ahead of TU's packaging roadmap.
